title: practical english usage - Part 198
content:
Whether we go by bus or train, it'll take at least six hours.

Present tenses are used to refer to the future.

The ticket will cost the same, whether we buy it now or wait till later.

to It doesn't matter whether... or...

\Ne can use whether... or... as a double conjunction, with a similar meaning 254 whether        ...  or grammar •  256  than- and ɑs -clauses: leaving out subjects, etc You're as tired as what I am. (non-standard) They sent more paper than what I had ordered. (non-standard) Some English dialects use what after as and than in these cases.

You're as tired as I am. (     ... as I am-it.) not She was more frightened than I was. (     . .. than I was it) not Don't lose your passport, as I did last year. (     ... as I did it last year.) ordered them.) not They sent more vegetables than I had ordered. (     ... than I had Than and as can also take the place of objects and complements.

2 objects      and   complements:         as   I did    last    year not As is well known, smoking is dangerous. (     As it is well-known...) not I am sending you the bill, as was agreed. (     ... as it was agreed.) not They lost money, as was expected. (     ... as it was expected.) not / have prepared a new plan, as follows. (    .. as it follows.) He said he was happy and didn't want to go home.

He was happy and he didn't want to go home.

the same as in other structures. Compare: special tenses or verb forms. She said to me, 'I've lost my keys'.

These two structures cannot normally be mixed.

And then I wondered whether he really meant it.

She asked what I wanted.

So he said that he wanted to go home, and just walked out.

This kind of structure is called ‘ indirect speech’ by using conjunctions with noun clauses, and changing pronouns, tenses and We   can also make somebody’ s words or thoughts part of our own sentence, And then 1 thought, 'Does he really mean it?' She asked, 'What do you want?' So he said, 'I want to go home,' and Just walked out.

well as speech).

of structure is called 'direct speech’  (though it is used for reporting thoughts as words (more or less) that were said, or that we  imagine were thought. This kind When  we  report people’ s words, thoughts, beliefs, etc, we  can give the exact direct     and   indirect      speech and whether.

They  are common in indirect speech, where they can also begin with if Noun clauses begin with that and with question-words like when, where or who.

The main thing is that you're happy, (noun clause complement) - The main thing is your happiness, (noun phrase complement) I told them that I knew nothing about it. (noun clause object) - / told them a lie. (noun phrase object) When we leave doesn't matter, (noun clause subject) - The departure date doesn't matter, (noun phrase subject) Clauses used like this are called 'noun clauses’ . Compare: Clauses can act as subjects, objects or complements, just like noun phrases.
